cardInfo.name: Kitchen Editorial
cardInfo.notes: Best paired with warm-toned food photography. Serif-heavy by design. The cream background reads particularly well on high-contrast displays. Dark mode shifts to a rich dark brown rather than pure black to preserve warmth throughout.
cardInfo.summary: Warm editorial theme inspired by Bon Appétit and NYT Cooking — cream pages, expressive serif type, terracotta warmth, and an intimate personal voice.

attributes.visualDNA: Editorial warmth: cream pages, ink-rich serif type, terracotta accents drawn from the spice rack. The mess-and-beauty aesthetic — imperfect composition, slightly generous whitespace, photography that shows texture and weight. Food-forward hierarchy: the dish is always the hero. Personal voice: the design implies someone wrote this, not an algorithm. Archival quality: this could exist beautifully in print. The through-line is appetite — everything should make the viewer hungry, curious, and welcome.

body.sampleText: This pasta came together on a Tuesday when I had nothing but ambition and a stick of butter. Brown it past the point where you think you should — that nutty, almost-smoky aroma is exactly what you're after.

caption.sampleText: Photo by Alex Lau / Bon Appétit

heading.sampleText: Browned Butter Pasta with Crispy Capers

attributes.colorPalette: An edible palette drawn from the pantry. Ivory cream anchors all backgrounds — warm enough to feel like paper, light enough to not compete with food photography. Deep burgundy provides editorial gravitas as the primary color. Terracotta-amber serves as the energetic accent, evoking saffron, paprika, and the golden crust of a well-baked loaf. Secondary tones echo wheat, aged parchment, and warm wood. The palette deliberately avoids any cool neutrals — every tone leans warm.
attributes.contentVoice: First-person, opinionated, and warmly conversational. 'I've made this seventeen times and every single time someone asks for the recipe.' Recipe headnotes tell a story before the first ingredient. Measurements are given in weight and volume, with notes on substitutions. The writing acknowledges failure: 'If your caramel seizes, don't panic.' Tone is never clinical, never algorithmic — it sounds like a friend who happens to cook very well.
inspirations: Bon Appétit magazine print issues (2015–2020), editorial photography and typographic hierarchy
NYT Cooking website and app, clean usability paired with personal editorial voice
Gourmet magazine archive, the authority and warmth of the original food publication
Salt Fat Acid Heat by Samin Nosrat, illustrated warmth and handwritten personality
Julia Turshen cookbooks, intimate voice and unpretentious visual presentation
attributes.designMindset: The kitchen as editorial space. This design is for people who love food deeply — who subscribe to BA, follow Samin Nosrat, dog-ear cookbooks, and have opinions about butter. The design should feel earned and intimate, like a recipe handed down on a handwritten index card. Every decision asks two questions: does this make you want to eat? And does it feel like someone who loves food made this? Aspiration and warmth are not opposites here — they're the same impulse.
attributes.motionLanguage: Quiet and purposeful — the motion of handling printed matter, not a screen. Hover transitions run at 150ms ease-out with no scale transforms. Page transitions feel like turning a page: a gentle fade, directional but not dramatic. Loading states use a slow warm pulse (2s ease-in-out). Step-through interactions slide in from the right at 200ms. Nothing bounces. Nothing scales. Motion serves clarity, not delight.
attributes.technicalSpecs: Base font size: 16px. Base line height: 1.75 for body, 1.15 for display headings. Max content width: 680px for prose, 920px for card grids. Image aspect ratios: 4:3 for thumbnails, 3:2 for feature cards, 1:1 for profile photos. Responsive breakpoints: 480px (single column), 768px (two column), 1024px (three column), 1280px (wide layout). Minimum tap target: 44×44px. Focus rings use the terracotta accent color.
attributes.compositionRules: Content-first with editorial breathing room. Recipe titles command the top at 42–48px. The hero image bleeds to the container edge with no padding. Ingredient lists are structured but gentle — casual enumeration, not clinical bullets. Instructions are written in generous prose with section breaks, never fragments. Metadata (cook time, servings, difficulty) appears as small-cap secondary text above the title. The asymmetry of a magazine layout is preferred over rigid grids.
attributes.qualityStandards: Every screen should feel like a page from a magazine you'd keep on the coffee table. Typography must never feel cramped — when in doubt, add space. Images must be warm-toned and never stretched; cold photography feels out of place. Interactive elements are obvious without being loud. If a component doesn't make you want to cook something, reconsider it. The test: would someone at Bon Appétit be proud to show this to their art director?
attributes.typographySystem: A deliberate dialogue between display serif and humanist sans. Headlines are set in Playfair Display — expressive, ink-on-paper, slightly theatrical with its high-contrast strokes. Long-form body and ingredient copy uses Lora for warmth and sustained readability. All utility text — tags, metadata, captions, labels — uses Inter at small sizes. Letter-spacing runs slightly tight on editorial serifs (-0.01em), generously loose on all-caps subheadings (0.12em). The system feels like a well-edited food magazine, not a recipe app.
attributes.geometricLanguage: Generous but not baroque. Cards use a 6px corner radius — worn paper edges rather than tech-product rounding. Dividers are single hairline rules in warm tan. Image frames respect natural crops without heavy masks or overlaid treatments. The overall composition is horizontal and editorial — magazine spread energy, not mobile app energy. Lists are spacious; buttons have breathing room; nothing is cramped.
attributes.historicalContext: Inspired by the golden age of food media — Gourmet magazine's editorial authority, Julia Child's intimate warmth, and the modern sensibility of Bon Appétit and NYT Cooking. This aesthetic crystallized in the early 2010s as food media shifted toward work that felt both aspirational and deeply personal: photography showing real kitchens, real mess, and real joy. The palette and typography draw from print tradition — cream stock, ink-rich serifs, and the warm amber of late-afternoon kitchen light.
attributes.materialVocabulary: Cream-coated paper stock. Cards feel like pages torn from a magazine — the background reads as soft uncoated newsprint. Shadows are diffuse and cast downward, simulating overhead kitchen light: no dramatic drop shadows, no deep vignettes. Hover states suggest the physical sensation of touching a glossy page — a subtle brightness shift rather than a scale transform. Depth is implied through layering and shadow, not color.
attributes.componentVocabulary: Tag chips are pill-shaped with warm ivory backgrounds and burgundy text. Primary buttons fill with deep burgundy — purposeful, editorial-weight. Secondary actions are text-links with a subtle underline on hover. Recipe cards lead with photography at 4:3 ratio, followed by a strong typographic block. Progress indicators for recipe steps are a thin terracotta bar. Star ratings use warm amber fill. Checkboxes in ingredient lists carry a soft check mark in burgundy.
attributes.applicationScenarios: Recipe cards with cook time, servings, and difficulty. Interactive ingredient checklists. Step-by-step cooking instruction sequences. Chef and author profile cards. Meal planning and weekly menu grids. Seasonal ingredient feature spreads. Restaurant and dish review cards. Food photography galleries with editorial captions.
